content:
Hey folks, I am very new to rust-for-linx and I'm trying to play around with writing an out-of-tree module. But what i have noticed is that the kernel crate documentation is different from the kernel API in rust-next branch, and more in sync with the archived rust branch.
So I am trying to figure out what is the recommended rust-for-linux/linux branch to use to build my kernel and play around?

See https://rust-for-linux.com/branches

I would recommend to build upon the rust-next branch. If you need stuff from the old rust branch, then you can try to port it to rust-next (of course you could also directly build on rust, but I would not recommend that, since it is so old).
if you don't need anything that is new this cycle, then you can also use any recent tag from torvalds.

Aswin Unnikrishnan said:

But what i have noticed is that the kernel crate documentation is different from the kernel API in rust-next branch, and more in sync with the archived rust branch.

what do you mean by kernel crate documentation?

Thanks, thats what I also assumed after reading the website.

what do you mean by kernel crate documentation?

I am referring to the rust api documentations that are linked on the website https://rust-for-linux.com/
can you directly link to the outdated docs?
Ah I see now
Im looking at this : https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/v6.8/kernel/
that is the up-to-date version

I've not gone through the whole stuff, but I am looking at this function : https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/v6.8/alloc/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.try_push

And I cant find it on rust-next : https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust-next/rust/kernel/alloc/vec_ext.rs
on the website there also is this link: rustdoc code docs (rust 2023-03-13) that leads to the docs of the rust branch

Aswin Unnikrishnan said:

I've not gone through the whole stuff, but I am looking at this function : https://rust-for-linux.github.io/docs/v6.8/alloc/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.try_push

And I cant find it on rust-next : https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust-next/rust/kernel/alloc/vec_ext.rs

that is because the docs are for v6.8 (the tag that you can find in torvalds' repo) and rust-next contains a patchset that changes the way we do allocations
note that you can also build the docs locally via make LLVM=1 rustdoc and then view it under Documentation/output/rust/rustdoc/kernel/index.html
Ohhh.. I see now.. That solves my issue then :)
So I am better off sticking to torvalds' repo since I am just starting off I guess

note that you can also build the docs locally via make LLVM=1 rustdoc and then view it under Documentation/output/rust/rustdoc/kernel/index.html

Okay, got it.
Thank you very much for the quick help !

Aswin Unnikrishnan said:

So I am better off sticking to torvalds' repo since I am just starting off I guess

if you don't need anything that is currently only available in rust-next, then it won't matter which one you use

Aswin Unnikrishnan said:

Thank you very much for the quick help !

No problem!

Could you also help me out with this..
So for the module! macro, In the initial documentation there is a mention of params field.
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/rust/macros/lib.rs#L38
However later on in the comment params is not mentioned as a supported argument type :
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/rust/macros/lib.rs#L70
It does not compile either if I give the params field. Is this a bug in the code comments? I did see that params was a thing in the rust branch and not in the rust-next branch
I'm guessing that was never upstreamed to Linux, but that it was accidentally included in the example.
Makes sense!
If you want, you can submit a patch that fixes the documentation.

That would be awesome!
Excuse me for the dumb question, but should I raise a PR to https://github.com/torvalds/linux or https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux
See https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing
Kernel development isn't done using github PRs
Oh, I gotta read up then! Sorry I'm very new and this is day 1 of trying to figure things out :)

We used to use github PRs for the old rust branch, but we don't anymore. Today, they're not used so much. Occassionally people will submit a PR as a place to get feedback before sending their change for real.
But I recommend that you just go straight to sending it to the mailing list, if you want to try it.
Start here: https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html
Okay, I will go through those. Thank you!!
Feel free to ask questions here. Getting started with sending patches to the kernel can be a bit daunting.

The description here says byte array , which is also wrong isn't it? It's been converted to string now.
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust-next/rust/macros/lib.rs#L72
Dropping the part about "byte array" seems reasonable to me.
Makes sense!

alias expects a string array : https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blame/rust-next/rust/macros/module.rs#L133

Any suggestion what could be an ideal alias I can provide in the example ? 

/// module!{
///     type: MyModule,
///     name: "my_kernel_module",
///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
///     license: "GPL",
///     alias: [""],
/// }
alternate_module_name
Perfect!

I just ran my patch through the checkpatch script and it warns me of missing commit description: 

ubuntu@rust-for-linux:~/linux$ perl scripts/checkpatch.pl --codespell --strict --fix-inplace 0001-Remove-nonexistent-argument-params-from-module-macro.patch
WARNING: Missing commit description - Add an appropriate one

total: 0 errors, 1 warnings, 0 checks, 35 lines checked

0001-Remove-nonexistent-argument-params-from-module-macro.patch has style problems, please review.

NOTE: If any of the errors are false positives, please report
      them to the maintainer, see CHECKPATCH in MAINTAINERS.
ubuntu@rust-for-linux:~/linux$
Well, what's your commit message?

git log : 

commit da795be5119b392352474adf5dbf894fd07f95d8 (HEAD -> master)
Author: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 19 15:38:31 2024 +0000

    Remove nonexistent argument params from module macro, Update documentation for module macro attribute types

    Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>

What am I missing :thinking:
Generally, a commit message has a title on the first line, then an empty line, then a multi-line description, another empty line, finally tags (e.g. Signed-off-by) at the end.
Ohh okay, got it :thumbs_up:

Here's one of my commits as an example:

commit 66c7392487bd70629f8ff54d6bda139083c34de7 (HEAD -> b4/alice-mm, origin/b4/alice-mm)
Author: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>
Date:   Tue Dec 12 17:25:30 2023 +0100

    rust: add abstraction for `struct page`

    Adds a new struct called `Page` that wraps a pointer to `struct page`.
    This struct is assumed to hold ownership over the page, so that Rust
    code can allocate and manage pages directly.

    The page type has various methods for reading and writing into the page.
    These methods will temporarily map the page to allow the operation. All
    of these methods use a helper that takes an offset and length, performs
    bounds checks, and returns a pointer to the given offset in the page.

    This patch only adds support for pages of order zero, as that is all
    Rust Binder needs. However, it is written to make it easy to add support
    for higher-order pages in the future. To do that, you would add a const
    generic parameter to `Page` that specifies the order. Most of the
    methods do not need to be adjusted, as the logic for dealing with
    mapping multiple pages at once can be isolated to just the
    `with_pointer_into_page` method.

    Rust Binder needs to manage pages directly as that is how transactions
    are delivered: Each process has an mmap'd region for incoming
    transactions. When an incoming transaction arrives, the Binder driver
    will choose a region in the mmap, allocate and map the relevant pages
    manually, and copy the incoming transaction directly into the page. This
    architecture allows the driver to copy transactions directly from the
    address space of one process to another, without an intermediate copy
    to a kernel buffer.

    This code is based on Wedson's page abstractions from the old rust
    branch, but it has been modified by Alice by removing the incomplete
    support for higher-order pages, by introducing the `with_*` helpers
    to consolidate the bounds checking logic into a single place, and
    various other changes.

    Co-developed-by: Wedson Almeida Filho <wedsonaf@gmail.com>
    Signed-off-by: Wedson Almeida Filho <wedsonaf@gmail.com>
    Reviewed-by: Andreas Hindborg <a.hindborg@samsung.com>
    Reviewed-by: Trevor Gross <tmgross@umich.edu>
    Reviewed-by: Benno Lossin <benno.lossin@proton.me>
    Signed-off-by: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>
So you'll want a shorter title and a description.
Got it. Updating!
Should I be adding Reviewed-by while creating my commit description?
No, those are in mine because those people have reviewed the commit when I previously sent it to the list
Okay

One thing to think about is that on GitHub you have a PR description where you can put additional information, so often people will not also put into in the commit. In the kernel, the commit message is the only place to put information.

Aswin Unnikrishnan said:

Should I be adding Reviewed-by while creating my commit description?

In general you can only add tags that mention other people if you have their permission to do so.
Understood.

Does this look better? 

commit 227044a6a4918f44a28cc061c410c673564ad929 (HEAD -> master)
Author: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 19 15:41:54 2024 +0000

    rust: fix documentation for module macro

    Removes nonexistent argument `params` from module macro example.
    Add an example for alias argument for module macro.
    Fix the documentation for datatypes of module macro arguments.

    Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Yes that's better
Thank you!
I'm on the way home, but I have some more feedback on the commit message that I'll share soon. But it is better now.
Okay, sure!

Usually commit messages are written in imperative mood, that is Add, Change, Fix, Remove rather than Adds, Changes, Fixes, Removes. (I know my commit does it wrong, oops.)

Another thing is that you're treating the newlines as significant, but that looks weird to me. Generally, you always continue the line until the length limit. If you want a line break, then add an empty line for a paragraph break. Or use a bulleted list.

Finally, your sentences are really just a list of changes, so they don't really fit together as a paragraph that you can read like an article... I don't know how to best explain. I might write:

Remove params from the documentation of the module! macro, as the macro doesn't actually support parameter lists right now. Also add an example for the alias parameter.

And finally, if you are including the word "also" in your commit, then maybe it should be multiple commits.

Thats very clearly explained! I think I have an understanding how to fix that.

And finally, if you are including the word "also" in your commit, then maybe it should be multiple commits.

Can multiple commits be pushed via the same patch ?
iirc from reading, I should add each commit as each patch, and the patch summary should say [Patch 1/2] <title>
You can send a patchset, which has one email per commit but they are sent together. (Each subsequent commit email is a reply to the first commit.)
The tool will take of the [PATCH] and numbering for you

[PATCH 1/3] rust: fix documentation for module macro
3 messages
Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>  20 April 2024 at 02:46
To: aswinunni01@gmail.com
Remove nonexistent argument `params` from module macro example,
because the macro does not support parameter list right now.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 12 ------------
 1 file changed, 12 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index f489f3157383..520eae5fd792 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -35,18 +35,6 @@
 ///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
 ///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
 ///     license: "GPL",
-///     params: {
-///        my_i32: i32 {
-///            default: 42,
-///            permissions: 0o000,
-///            description: "Example of i32",
-///        },
-///        writeable_i32: i32 {
-///            default: 42,
-///            permissions: 0o644,
-///            description: "Example of i32",
-///        },
-///    },
 /// }
 ///
 /// struct MyModule;
--
2.34.1

Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>  20 April 2024 at 02:46
To: aswinunni01@gmail.com
Add example for alias argument supported by module macro.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index 520eae5fd792..aa89b41fa10e 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -35,6 +35,7 @@
 ///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
 ///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
 ///     license: "GPL",
+///     alias: ["alternate_module_name"],
[Quoted text hidden]
Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>  20 April 2024 at 02:46
To: aswinunni01@gmail.com
Remove the mention of byte array as datatype for `module` macro arguments
since the arguments are defined as string, and `alias` is a string array.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 10 +++++-----
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index aa89b41fa10e..da7cd6378288 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -58,11 +58,11 @@
 ///
 /// # Supported argument types
 ///   - `type`: type which implements the [`Module`] trait (required).
-///   - `name`: byte array of the name of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `author`: byte array of the author of the kernel module.
-///   - `description`: byte array of the description of the kernel module.
-///   - `license`: byte array of the license of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `alias`: byte array of alias name of the kernel module.
+///   - `name`: name of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `author`: author of the kernel module.
+///   - `description`: description of the kernel module.
+///   - `license`: license of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `alias`: array of alias names of the kernel module.
 #[proc_macro]
 pub fn module(ts: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
     module::module(ts)
--

Actually Gmail has done some formatting on this like

[Quoted text hidden]

Let me see if I can get a better output
What are you using to send it?

git format-patch 2668e3ae2ef36d5e7c52f818ad7d90822c037de4
git send-email --annotate --to="aswinunni01@gmail.com" *.patch
The formatting might be weird because I copied it directly from gmail
You can just share the contents of the file that format-patch generated. (for review here, that is - you still need to send a real email afterwards)
But that looks reasonable for actually sending it as an email.

ubuntu@rust-for-linux:~/linux$ cat *.patch
From 05f9a1f8820ed81f35a82e9e67a0916a9f828764 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 20:44:38 +0000
Subject: [PATCH 1/3] rust: fix documentation for module macro

Remove nonexistent argument `params` from module macro example,
because the macro does not support parameter list right now.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 12 ------------
 1 file changed, 12 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index f489f3157383..520eae5fd792 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -35,18 +35,6 @@
 ///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
 ///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
 ///     license: "GPL",
-///     params: {
-///        my_i32: i32 {
-///            default: 42,
-///            permissions: 0o000,
-///            description: "Example of i32",
-///        },
-///        writeable_i32: i32 {
-///            default: 42,
-///            permissions: 0o644,
-///            description: "Example of i32",
-///        },
-///    },
 /// }
 ///
 /// struct MyModule;
--
2.34.1

From 4121ace49a327bb595e2e356dcf24704abc1657c Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 20:53:17 +0000
Subject: [PATCH 2/3] rust: fix documentation for module macro

Add example for alias argument supported by module macro.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index 520eae5fd792..aa89b41fa10e 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -35,6 +35,7 @@
 ///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
 ///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
 ///     license: "GPL",
+///     alias: ["alternate_module_name"],
 /// }
 ///
 /// struct MyModule;
--
2.34.1

From ac747e1901d8fef196356303976cce2ec980dae6 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 20:57:38 +0000
Subject: [PATCH 3/3] rust: fix documentation for module macro

Remove the mention of byte array as datatype for `module` macro arguments
since the arguments are defined as string, and `alias` is a string array.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 10 +++++-----
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index aa89b41fa10e..da7cd6378288 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -58,11 +58,11 @@
 ///
 /// # Supported argument types
 ///   - `type`: type which implements the [`Module`] trait (required).
-///   - `name`: byte array of the name of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `author`: byte array of the author of the kernel module.
-///   - `description`: byte array of the description of the kernel module.
-///   - `license`: byte array of the license of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `alias`: byte array of alias name of the kernel module.
+///   - `name`: name of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `author`: author of the kernel module.
+///   - `description`: description of the kernel module.
+///   - `license`: license of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `alias`: array of alias names of the kernel module.
 #[proc_macro]
 pub fn module(ts: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
     module::module(ts)
--
2.34.1

From 05f9a1f8820ed81f35a82e9e67a0916a9f828764 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From 4121ace49a327bb595e2e356dcf24704abc1657c Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From ac747e1901d8fef196356303976cce2ec980dae6 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001

Are these okay?
Please use different titles for the commits. Otherwise looks good to me.
Using different commit titles causes them to come as 3 different emails with different subject rather than as replies. Is that okay?
Gmail will show them separately, but they will still be replies.
E.g., thunderbird will show them in the right way.
Oh okay, will do it then

ubuntu@rust-for-linux:~/linux$ git log
commit b82275f6e582c573f6d8afe9bc7ad8da0a9b0e8a (HEAD -> master)
Author: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 19 20:57:38 2024 +0000

    rust: fix documentation for module macro arguments

    Remove the mention of byte array as datatype for `module` macro arguments
    since the arguments are defined as string, and `alias` is a string array.

    Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>

commit af2d43626b7f3101fa3d7f0514ae24c94f8a39f4
Author: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 19 20:53:17 2024 +0000

    rust: update documentation for module macro

    Add example for `alias` argument supported by module macro.

    Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>

commit de6548fed99fe08f1967942067ecc643ad27c212
Author: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Apr 19 20:44:38 2024 +0000

    rust: fix example for module macro

    Remove nonexistent argument `params` from `module` macro example,
    because the macro does not support parameter list right now.

    Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Let me make a small change
You're allowed to be more precise and say e.g. "rust: remove params from module macro docs"
Oh okay : ) , I was just worried that my description would be almost repetitive to the title.
I mean, in the case of adding alias, you could give a short explanation of what it does.
In general, most changes should contain a justification for why the change is a good idea ... but for docs changes it's pretty obvious. Good docs are good.
Got it

Remove nonexistent argument `params` from `module` macro example,
because the macro does not support parameter list right now.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>

-///            default: 42,

-///            description: "Example of i32",
-///        },
-///        writeable_i32: i32 {
-///            default: 42,
-///            permissions: 0o644,

From e999c6f2dcdb6944421e6de73d5da350081efed6 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 20:53:17 +0000
Subject: [PATCH 2/3] rust: add example for `alias` argument in `module` macro
 documentation

Add example for `alias` argument supported by `module` macro.
`alias` accepts an array of alternate names for the module as string.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---

 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index 520eae5fd792..aa89b41fa10e 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -35,6 +35,7 @@
 ///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
 ///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
 ///     license: "GPL",
+///     alias: ["alternate_module_name"],
 /// }
 ///
 /// struct MyModule;
--
2.34.1

From c90530ae1fd291d31f4e7ac36fcbc172d8793f86 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 20:57:38 +0000
Subject: [PATCH 3/3] rust: fix datatype in docs for `module` macro arguments

Remove the mention of byte array as datatype for `module` macro arguments
since the arguments are defined as string, and `alias` is a string array.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 10 +++++-----
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index aa89b41fa10e..da7cd6378288 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -58,11 +58,11 @@
 ///
 /// # Supported argument types
 ///   - `type`: type which implements the [`Module`] trait (required).
-///   - `name`: byte array of the name of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `author`: byte array of the author of the kernel module.
-///   - `description`: byte array of the description of the kernel module.
-///   - `license`: byte array of the license of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `alias`: byte array of alias name of the kernel module.
+///   - `name`: name of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `author`: author of the kernel module.
+///   - `description`: description of the kernel module.
+///   - `license`: license of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `alias`: array of alias names of the kernel module.
 #[proc_macro]
 pub fn module(ts: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
     module::module(ts)
--
2.34.1
lgtm

Thank you!

I have generated the email recipients using 

awk '/^RUST$/,/^$/' MAINTAINERS | grep '^M:'    | cut -f2- | xargs -IP echo --to \'P\' \\
awk '/^RUST$/,/^$/' MAINTAINERS | grep '^[RL]:' | cut -f2- | xargs -IP echo --cc \'P\' \\

Does this command look good (--dry-run only for testing) 

git send-email \
--dry-run \
--annotate \
--to 'Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>' \
--to 'Alex Gaynor <alex.gaynor@gmail.com>' \
--to 'Wedson Almeida Filho <wedsonaf@gmail.com>' \
--cc 'Boqun Feng <boqun.feng@gmail.com>' \
--cc 'Gary Guo <gary@garyguo.net>' \
--cc 'Björn Roy Baron <bjorn3_gh@protonmail.com>' \

So I have a review comment on my patch : 

I think the original intention here was to document mainly the "type"
of the arguments (given the title of the section), i.e. the kind of
values one can write for each key.

So it would perhaps be best to give that information, e.g. "ASCII
string literal ...". Please see the code of the macro to see which
type is required for each field (e.g. is it a literal? Does it require
ASCII? ...?).

This should probably be considered a fix, so could you please add a
`Fixes:` tag and a `Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org` one in v2 (since it
does not hurt)? See e.g. what I did for your other fix I applied
already to `rust-fixes` (but note it fixes a different commit) and
https://docs.kernel.org/process/submitting-patches.html#describe-your-changes.

Thanks!

Cheers,
Miguel

Can someone please guide me, how I should be creating a new patch that addresses this comment?
Should I create a new commit and use git format-patch again on that particular commit?
Yes, just with git format-patch -v2 ... so you get the [PATCH v2] header
Recommend specifying an output directory like -o ../patches/my-branch-name-v2 so your files don't get mixed up

And then edit the cover letter patch to add a "Changes in v2" section after the --- but before the diffstat (this area exists in the emails but gets trimmed from the git messages). Example: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240328013603.206764-1-wedsonaf@gmail.com/
Okay, thanks!
https://git-send-email.io is a pretty great tutorial by the way, makes you send a patch to a bot that requests changes and has you send a v2. Kind of funny lol

When you send the next version, you can include the Reviewed-by tags that you got from the previous version in your commit message right before your signed-off-by
(But you should only include them if you have not made any important changes.)

Alice Ryhl said:

(But you should only include them if you have not made any important changes.)

Is that because adding the Reviewed-by tag means its not going to be reviewed again?

If you look in the kernel documentation about submitting patches, you will find an explanation of what Reviewed-by means.

Reviewer’s statement of oversight

By offering my Reviewed-by: tag, I state that:

  1. I have carried out a technical review of this patch to evaluate its appropriateness and readiness for inclusion into the mainline kernel.
  2. Any problems, concerns, or questions relating to the patch have been communicated back to the submitter. I am satisfied with the submitter’s response to my comments.
  3. While there may be things that could be improved with this submission, I believe that it is, at this time, (1) a worthwhile modification to the kernel, and (2) free of known issues which would argue against its inclusion.
  4. While I have reviewed the patch and believe it to be sound, I do not (unless explicitly stated elsewhere) make any warranties or guarantees that it will achieve its stated purpose or function properly in any given situation.

A Reviewed-by tag is a statement of opinion that the patch is an appropriate modification of the kernel without any remaining serious technical issues. Any interested reviewer (who has done the work) can offer a Reviewed-by tag for a patch. This tag serves to give credit to reviewers and to inform maintainers of the degree of review which has been done on the patch. Reviewed-by: tags, when supplied by reviewers known to understand the subject area and to perform thorough reviews, will normally increase the likelihood of your patch getting into the kernel.

So if you send a patch containing Reviewed-by: Alice Ryhl <aliceryhl@google.com>, then _you_ are saying that _I_ am saying the above about your patch.
But if the patch has changed since I sent a Reviewed-by:, then that could have changed my opinion.

And so it's really about whether you are misrepresenting what other people have said or not. If you just fixed a typo, I'm probably still happy. If you reworded the entire thing, you should probably let me review again.
Does that make sense?

Ohkay, makes sense. In this case, I am rewording the datatype documentation, based on the comment for my 3rd Patch : https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240419215015.157258-3-aswinunni01@gmail.com/T/#me46729ff94b3cb3ab49b18e3c529249aefed3107

The changes I am planning is here : https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/291565-Help/topic/rust-next.20vs.20rust.20branch/near/436083422
So I think it does qualify for a review again.

I'll make the change in a bit because I just lost my dev setup and my VM.

And so it's really about whether you are misrepresenting what other people have said or not. If you just fixed a typo, I'm probably still happy. If you reworded the entire thing, you should probably let me review again.

I get the point tho. For minor fixes, we don't need to go for reviews again.
Exactly.
Similarly, if my patchset has 3 patches, then I might send a new version where I only changed one of them.
And then I might remove Reviewed-by tags on only the one I changed.
It's also possible that someone might say "if you make this change, then you can add my Reviewed-by"

Note that the changelog doesn't go in the commit description itself, but below the --- line in the email. That way, they show up in the list but not in the final commit history when it gets merged.
You can only add it there by modifying the .patch files generated by format-patch.
Ohhh.. I added --- myself into the commit description :) . So I have to git commit -a --amend without modifying the commit message.

ubuntu@ubuntu:~/Desktop/linux$ cat ../patches/v2*
From 583e1a4abd0df2db22dcdb8e1aa283ac83918ae4 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 21:50:14 +0000
Subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] rust: add example for `alias` argument in `module`
 macro documentation

Add example for `alias` argument supported by `module` macro.
`alias` accepts an array of alternate names for the module as string.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---
 rust/macros/lib.rs | 1 +
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index 520eae5fd792..aa89b41fa10e 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -35,6 +35,7 @@
 ///     author: "Rust for Linux Contributors",
 ///     description: "My very own kernel module!",
 ///     license: "GPL",
+///     alias: ["alternate_module_name"],
 /// }
 ///
 /// struct MyModule;
--
2.43.0

From 8adaef5e744c524a302d45303ebdd7c14d38ef57 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Apr 2024 21:50:15 +0000
Subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] rust: fix datatype in docs for `module` macro
 arguments

Remove the mention of byte array as datatype for `module` macro arguments
since the arguments are defined as string, and `alias` is a string array.

Signed-off-by: Aswin Unnikrishnan <aswinunni01@gmail.com>
---

Changes in v2:
- Add expected string literal type in documentation for `module` macro
  arguments since some `module` macro arguments expect ASCII string
  literals.

 rust/macros/lib.rs | 10 +++++-----
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/macros/lib.rs b/rust/macros/lib.rs
index aa89b41fa10e..5214e07367c5 100644
--- a/rust/macros/lib.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/lib.rs
@@ -58,11 +58,11 @@
 ///
 /// # Supported argument types
 ///   - `type`: type which implements the [`Module`] trait (required).
-///   - `name`: byte array of the name of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `author`: byte array of the author of the kernel module.
-///   - `description`: byte array of the description of the kernel module.
-///   - `license`: byte array of the license of the kernel module (required).
-///   - `alias`: byte array of alias name of the kernel module.
+///   - `name`: ASCII string literal of the name of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `author`: string literal of the author of the kernel module.
+///   - `description`: string literal of the description of the kernel module.
+///   - `license`: ASCII string literal of the license of the kernel module (required).
+///   - `alias`: array of ASCII string literals of the alias names of the kernel module.
 #[proc_macro]
 pub fn module(ts: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
     module::module(ts)
--
2.43.0
